name: refried beans
instructions:
Sauté white onions and garlic in lard until softened. Transfer to a blender with stock and blend smooth — this step is non-optional, blended onions are the structural backbone of the dish.

Return the blended onion-and-stock mixture to the pan with the pinto beans. Add cumin, bay leaves (and new mexican chili powder if using). Cook down, mashing the beans as you go, until you reach the texture you want. Add more lard as needed for flavor and richness. Remove the bay leaves before serving.

Finish: salt and black pepper to taste. Fold in a melting cheese (monterrey jack, colby jack, or similar) if going the cheese route.
notes:
BLENDED ONIONS ARE NON-OPTIONAL: the onions get sautéed first, then blended with stock before they meet the beans. This is the structural step — chunky diced onions in refried beans read off, and skipping the blend leaves the dish flat. The blended onion-and-stock paste is what gives the beans body and aromatic depth.

STOCK, NOT BOUILLON: refried beans are a bean-led dish and want the gentler, more layered body of liquid stock. Bouillon cubes are too concentrated and salty here — see the bouillon-vs-stock article.

LARD IS THE FAT: this is what makes the dish read as proper refried beans rather than 'mashed beans with onion.' Don't substitute neutral oil or butter — lard's flavor is doing real work. Add more than you think you need.

NEW MEXICAN CHILI POWDER (OPTIONAL): a small amount adds warmth and color without changing the dish's identity. Optional but recommended.

MELTING CHEESE (OPTIONAL): if adding cheese, use a melting cheese — monterrey jack, colby jack, or similar mild meltable cheese. NOT sharp cheddar: sharp cheddar clashes with the bean-and-lard base, dominates the flavor, and reads anglo/american rather than mexican.

LIME (USE SPARINGLY): I had lime as clashing originally, but the nisha reference recipe (below) uses lime juice at the end and that may be a useful corrective for lighter-fat versions of the dish. Keeping it as a secondary — refried beans are a starchy savory base, not a brightness-and-acid dish, so lime can throw the balance off if used heavily. If used at all, a small amount stirred in off-heat. Not the default move with the canonical lard version.

=== REFERENCE RECIPE — NISHA VORA'S VEGAN REFRIED BEANS (TO TRY) ===

From rainbow plant life (vegan quesadillas with smoky cheese and refried beans post). A vegan version that uses oil instead of lard. Worth trying as a no-lard alternative — interesting to see how the absence of lard's structural flavor reshapes the recipe. Captured here as a reference, not a replacement for the canonical lard version.

INGREDIENTS:
- 1 tbsp olive oil or neutral oil
- 2 garlic cloves, minced
- 1 tsp dried oregano
- 1 tsp chili powder
- 1/2 tsp cumin
- 1 (15-oz / 440g) can pinto beans OR black beans, drained and rinsed
- 2 bay leaves
- 2/3 - 1 cup (160-240 mL) water, vegetable broth, or canned bean liquid
- kosher salt, black pepper to taste
- 1 tbsp fresh lime juice (or lemon juice, or half the amount of white/cider vinegar)
- 1 small handful cilantro, chopped (optional)

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Heat oil in a large frying pan over medium heat. Add garlic, cook 1-2 min stirring frequently until golden brown.
2. Add oregano, chili powder, cumin. Cook 1 min stirring frequently. Add drained beans, bay leaves, and broth/water. Cook 5-10 min until warmed through. Remove bay leaves.
3. Use an immersion blender or potato masher to mash most of the beans, leaving some whole for texture. Add more broth/water if dry. Continue cooking 2-4 more min, stirring often.
4. Season with salt and pepper. Off heat, stir in lime juice and cilantro (if using).

NOTABLE DIFFERENCES FROM THE CANONICAL VERSION (worth studying):
- NO LARD: uses olive/neutral oil. The whole point of this version. Worth tasting to see how much of the canonical version's character is lard-specific.
- NO BLENDED ONIONS — in fact, no onion at all in the bean component itself (in nisha's quesadilla context, sautéed onions are a SEPARATE component that goes on top). For a refried-beans-as-the-dish use case, you'd likely want to add onion back in.
- ADDS LIME JUICE AT THE END: directly contradicts the NO LIME rule above. Worth testing whether this is a real disagreement or whether the lime is doing different work in a lard-free version (i.e. maybe lime substitutes for the missing fat-flavor by providing brightness instead).
- BAY LEAVES: simmered with the beans for a few minutes then removed. New technique to try.
- GENERIC CHILI POWDER, NOT NEW MEXICAN: nisha uses generic chili powder. Probably substitutable with new mexican.
- BLACK BEANS AS AN ALTERNATIVE: nisha explicitly allows black beans alongside pinto. Worth considering for variety even in the lard version.
- BEAN-COOKING LIQUID: nisha allows the canned bean liquid as the cooking liquid (the goopy starchy can liquid). Free flavor and body if you trust the can.

Things to learn from this recipe: whether the lime-at-the-end works in the lard-free context, whether bay leaves add useful body to a refried beans simmer, and how much of the canonical version's identity is specifically lard-dependent vs general bean-mash mechanics.
